1:
"In those days came John the Baptist, preaching in the wilderness of Judaea." The witnesses read the appearance of the forerunner as the dawning of the gospel day, and weigh the loose dating "in those days."

The Latin fathers expound the office and its symbolism. Pseudo-Chrysostom: as the sun "sends out his rays" before he is visible, so the forerunner goes before Christ. Remigius finds here "time, place, and person... his office and employment." Gregory draws the great contrast: "the Only-begotten Son is called the Word of the Father" (John 1:1), and John "only is called the Voice," the voice that goes before the Word. Bede: God "has cried from the beginning through the voice of all who have spoken by inspiration," yet John is pre-eminently "the voice."

The Eastern fathers fix the chronology and the witness. Chrysostom asks why John must "go before Christ with a witness of deeds," answering that we might learn Christ's dignity. John Chrysostom and Augustine note "those days" cannot mean Christ's infancy (ch. 2) but a time some thirty years later (Luke 3:1).

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers concur. Robertson: "the synoptic narrative begins with the baptism of John" (Mark 1:2; Luke 3:1; Acts 1:22). Barnes: "in those days" extends over "the whole time that Jesus dwelt at Nazareth." Calvin and the ancients agree John was "about thirty years of age." Wesley fixes the "wilderness of Judaea" as a wild, desolate place. So the witnesses agree: after the long silence since the infancy narrative, the forerunner steps forth in the desert — the Voice preparing the way of the Word, and the gospel history properly begins (v.2).

So the witnesses set the chapter as the dawn of the gospel: after the long silence since the infancy, the forerunner steps from the desert, the Voice before the Word, and the Synoptic history begins. The fathers' image of the herald's light before sunrise and Calvin's note that John was about thirty together frame the appearing of the man sent to prepare the Lord's way (v.3).
2:
"Repent ye: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand." The witnesses read the substance of John's preaching — and the much-disputed meaning of "repent."

The grammatical-historical readers press the word. Robertson: the English "repent" is "the worst translation in the New Testament" — John "did not call on the people to be sorry, but to change (think afterwards) their mental attitude" (metanoeite). Clarke: after such preaching "the sinner is led to understand" his way was "the way of misery, death, and hell." Ellicott: the Greek "implies change of mind and purpose," the English root-sense of pain being "hardly adequate." Barnes keeps both — "sorrow for past offences" (2 Corinthians 7:10) "and a full purpose to turn from transgression."

The Reformed voice and Lightfoot read the setting. Lightfoot: a nation "leavened with the error of the Pharisees, concerning justification by the works of the law, was necessarily to be called off to the contrary doctrine." Calvin notes Matthew gives "the substance of John's doctrine" in John's own words.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley fixes the kingdom: "the kingdom of heaven and the kingdom of God are but two phrases for the same thing," meaning "not barely a future happy state in heaven, but a state to be enjoyed on earth" — "the proper disposition for the glory of heaven." So the witnesses agree: John's cry is for a real change of mind and life, not mere sorrow, urged by the nearness of the kingdom God is about to bring — the message Christ himself will take up (Matthew 4:17). The repentance demanded is the turning of the whole man toward a kingdom now drawing near.

So the witnesses make repentance the heart of the message — not mere sorrow but the turning of the whole mind, urged by the nearness of the kingdom. Robertson's insistence on 'change of mind' and Wesley's that the kingdom is a state to be enjoyed on earth together press the call that Christ himself will take up word for word (Matthew 4:17).

The repentance demanded is the turning of the whole man toward a kingdom now drawing near (v.3).
3:
"For this is he that was spoken of by the prophet Esaias, The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord." The witnesses read the Evangelist's own application of Isaiah 40:3 to the Baptist.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers fix the citation and the speaker. Gill and Ellicott: "these are not the words of the Baptist himself" (though he used them of his own office, John 1:23) "but of the Evangelist," citing Isaiah "very pertinently, since that chapter is a prophecy of the Messiah." Barnes: the language "was familiar to the Jews." Clarke: the image is taken from the practice of eastern monarchs, whose coming was prepared by a crier.

Calvin guards the breadth of the prophecy: it "ought not to be limited exclusively to John, yet he is one of the number of those to whom it certainly refers" — Isaiah, after the calamities of the people, "promises a restoration" of which the Baptist's cry is the herald.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the preparation: "make his paths straight, by removing every thing which might prove a hindrance to his gracious appearance." So the witnesses agree: John is the fulfilment of Isaiah's prophesied voice — the herald who, in the wilderness, prepares the Lord's way by the preaching of repentance. The Evangelist anchors the new gospel in the old prophecy, so that the forerunner's strange desert ministry is seen to be no novelty but the appointed approach of the long-promised Messiah (v.2).

So the witnesses anchor the new gospel in the old prophecy: John is the very voice Isaiah foresaw, preparing the Lord's way in the wilderness. Calvin's care that the oracle is not limited to John yet certainly refers to him, and the moderns' note that these are the Evangelist's own words, together show the forerunner as the appointed herald of the long-promised Messiah (v.11).

The forerunner's strange desert ministry is thus seen to be no novelty but the appointed approach of the long-promised Messiah (v.4).

The Evangelist anchors the new gospel in the old prophecy, so that nothing in John's ministry is without its appointed place in Scripture (Isa 40:3).
4:
"And the same John had his raiment of camel's hair, and a leathern girdle about his loins; and his meat was locusts and wild honey." The witnesses read the forerunner's austere dress and food as a sermon in themselves.

The Latin fathers read the symbolism. Pseudo-Chrysostom: the description shows "what his life was." Jerome: "raiment of camel's hair, not of wool — the one the mark of austerity, the other of delicate luxury," with food "suited to a dweller in the desert." Remigius: in this poor clothing and food "he shews that he sorrows for the sins of the whole human race." Hilary: "no clothing more useful, no food more fitted" for the preacher of Christ. Rabanus: "content with poor fare."

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers fix the Elijah parallel. Clarke: "a sort of coarse covering, common to the prophets" (Zechariah 13:4), in which Elijah was clothed (2 Kings 1:8), and John, designed under that name (Malachi 4:5), bore his spirit. Barnes: not fine camlet but "the long, shaggy hair of the camel." Wesley: "like Elijah, in whose spirit and power he came"; locusts "are ranked among clean meats" (Leviticus 11:22). Lightfoot adds the Talmudic note on locusts fit for food.

The Reformed voice guards against mistaking the point. Calvin: the Evangelist does not reckon "a rough and austere way of living" among John's "chief excellencies," but, having shown he dwelt in the mountains, notes that his food was such as the place afforded. So the witnesses agree: John's garb and diet mark him as the new Elijah and the preacher of penitence — his very appearance proclaiming the wilderness life and the call to repentance, though his greatness lay not in austerity itself but in his office as the Lord's forerunner (v.3).

So the witnesses read John's garb and food as a sermon: the new Elijah, clad in austerity, preaching penitence by his very appearance. The fathers' symbolism of sorrow for the human race and Calvin's caution that austerity was not his chief excellency together keep the balance — the dress marks the man, but his greatness lay in his office as forerunner (v.5).
5:
"Then went out to him Jerusalem, and all Judaea, and all the region round about Jordan." The witnesses read the great multitudes drawn to the desert preacher.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the attraction. Pseudo-Chrysostom: "his severe life preached yet more loudly in the desert than the voice of his preaching." Chrysostom: it drew the Jews to see "in him the great Elias," the grace of prophecy having long failed. Remigius likens John's baptism to the catechizing of children before the sacrament; the Gloss notes "this baptism was only a forerunning of that to come, and did not forgive sins." Rabanus: rightly are the baptized said to "go out" to the prophet, for they depart from sin.

The grammatical-historical readers gauge the scope. Barnes: "not literally all the people," but "great multitudes." Ellicott: "all the region round about Jordan" would include "parts of Peraea, Samaria, Galilee, and Gaulonitis." Lightfoot weighs the geography of the "region round about."

Gill gathers the causes — "the uncommon appearance of this person, the oddness of his dress, the austerity of his life," the awe of his doctrine, the novelty of baptism, and the long absence of any prophet. So the witnesses agree: the desert preacher drew the whole land — Jerusalem, Judaea, and the Jordan valley — by the very strangeness and severity of his life and the long-awaited renewal of prophecy. The crowds came confessing their sins to a baptism that, the fathers note, was preparatory and not yet the forgiving baptism of Christ (v.6).

So the witnesses gather the causes of the great conflux: the strangeness and severity of John's life, the long-failed grace of prophecy revived, and the expectation of the near Messiah. The fathers' note that his baptism was preparatory and not yet forgiving, and the moderns' on the breadth of the region, together set the scene of a whole land going out to confess its sins (v.6).

The crowds came confessing to a baptism that, the fathers note, was preparatory and not yet the forgiving baptism of Christ (v.11).

The long absence of any prophet only deepened the wonder of his appearing.
6:
"And were baptized of him in Jordan, confessing their sins." The witnesses read the rite and its accompanying confession.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the act. Barnes: the word "originally to tinge, to dye, to stain... here means to cleanse or wash by the application of water," ablution being "much in use among the Jews" (Hebrews 9:10). Robertson: the imperfect "were baptized" shows "the repetition of the act as the crowds kept going out." Ellicott: the tense "implies continual succession — crowd after crowd passed on," confessing "in the closest possible connection with the act of immersion."

The mode is disputed. Clarke weighs dip versus sprinkle — "can any man suppose that it was possible for John to dip all" — and Wesley doubts "such prodigious numbers" could be immersed, with no change of raiment. Others read immersion from the river setting. Lightfoot asks why so great a conflux, answering that "the manifestation of the Messias was then expected, the weeks of Daniel being now" near their term (Daniel 9).

The Reformed voice reads the mutual pledge. Calvin: as God in the sacraments "brings himself under obligation to us... so it is our duty, on the other hand, to reply to him" — by confession of sin answering his offer of forgiveness. So the witnesses agree: the multitudes were baptized in Jordan with open confession of sin — a rite of cleansing and a pledge of repentance, whatever its precise mode — the people responding to John's call in expectation of the near Messiah (v.11).

So the witnesses read the baptism as a rite of cleansing and a pledge of repentance, whatever its precise mode. Calvin's image of God pledging himself in the sacrament while we reply by confession, and Lightfoot's that the conflux owed to the expected Messiah, together frame the multitudes' open confession of sin as a response to John's call (v.11).

The people responded to John's call in expectation of the near Messiah, the weeks of Daniel being now near their term (Dan 9).

The rite of cleansing was a pledge of the repentance John demanded.
7:
"But when he saw many of the Pharisees and Sadducees come to his baptism, he said unto them, O generation of vipers..." The witnesses read John's sharp word to the religious leaders, and supply the history of the two sects.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the discernment and the name. Pseudo-Chrysostom: "as a skilful physician from the colour of the skin infers the disease, so John understood the evil thoughts of the Pharisees." Remigius: Scripture names "from the imitation of deeds" (Ezekiel 16:3) — these are "vipers" from their venomous following of their fathers. Gregory: "the words of the teachers should be fitted to the quality of the hearers." Augustine: God's "wrath" is named "from likeness of effects, not from being subject to passion."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers describe the sects. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Lightfoot, and Wesley give the standard account — the Pharisees ("Separatists," outwardly strict, rigorous in tradition) and the Sadducees (denying resurrection and spirit). Robertson notes these "rival parties do not often unite" but do here and at Matthew 16:1: "a strong attraction" made them "forget their differences" against John.

The Puritan Henry: the doctrine of repentance now finds "the use of that doctrine" in application. Calvin: John "did not merely preach repentance in a general manner, but applied his discourse to individuals." So the witnesses agree: John, reading their hypocrisy, brands the Pharisees and Sadducees a "brood of vipers" — the preacher of repentance refusing to flatter the leaders, and warning them, with the same severity, of the wrath to come (v.8).

So the witnesses make this the application of John's doctrine to the leaders: reading their hypocrisy, he brands them a brood of vipers and warns of wrath. The fathers' image of the physician discerning the hidden disease, and the moderns' history of the rival sects united against him, together show the preacher of repentance refusing to flatter the great (v.8).

The preacher of repentance refuses to flatter the leaders, warning them, with the same severity, of the wrath to come (v.8).

His severity toward the great is of a piece with his whole prophetic calling.
8:
"Bring forth therefore fruits meet for repentance." The witnesses read John's demand that profession be proved by life.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the sense. Robertson: "John demands proof from these men of the new life before he administers baptism"; "the fruit is not the change of heart, but the acts which result from it." Ellicott corrects the ambiguous English: not fruit "preparing the way for repentance," but "bring forth fruit worthy of repentance" — the visible evidence of an inward turning. Barnes: "evince your sincerity by forsaking sins... carry not your hypocrisy into your professed repentance."

The Reformed voice presses the proof. Calvin: "repentance, which is attested by words, is of no value, unless it be proved by the conduct"; the "solemn declaration" is "not enough."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley distinguishes two repentances: "legal" repentance, "a thorough conviction of sin" (here in view), and "evangelical" repentance, "a change of heart and consequently of life from all sin to all holiness." So the witnesses agree: John will not baptize on a bare confession but demands the fruit that proves the root — works answering to repentance, lest the leaders bring their hypocrisy to the water. The call exposes any repentance that is merely verbal, and binds profession inseparably to amendment of life (v.10).

So the witnesses bind profession inseparably to amendment of life: John will not baptize on a bare confession but demands the fruit that proves the root. Calvin's that words without conduct are worthless, and Wesley's distinction of legal from evangelical repentance, together expose any repentance that is merely verbal (v.10).

The call binds profession inseparably to amendment of life, exposing any repentance that is merely verbal. John will not baptize on a bare confession, but demands the fruit that proves the root (v.9).

The leaders are warned not to carry their hypocrisy into the very waters of repentance.

The root must show itself in the branches, or the profession is empty before God (Lk 3:8).

John thus refuses to baptize a repentance that lives only on the lips, and not in the conduct.
9:
"And think not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father: for I say unto you, that God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham." The witnesses read John's blow against presumption on descent.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the boast. Robertson: "John touched the tender spot, their ecclesiastical pride" — they felt "the merits of the fathers, especially Abraham, were enough." Ellicott: the boast (as John 8:33-39) was joined to the creed "the Lord our God is one Lord" (Deuteronomy 6:4) as enough to ensure salvation. Barnes: they deemed descent "such an honour that it would go far to justify" them, but John "assured them that this was a matter of small consequence." Lightfoot notes "think not to say" is a Talmudic idiom.

The fathers and Jerome read the stones. Jerome: "these stones signify the Gentiles because of their hardness" — God who "made all things out of nothing" can make "men out of the hardest stone." The promise to Abraham is thus opened to a people raised by grace, not descent.

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices name the disease. Calvin: "till hypocrites are hard pressed, they either sleep in their sins or indulge in licentious mirth." Wesley: "it is almost incredible how great the presumption of the Jews was" on their descent. So the witnesses agree: John demolishes the false security of physical descent from Abraham — God can raise true children of Abraham even from stones (the Gentiles), so that birth avails nothing without the fruit of repentance (v.8).

So the witnesses see John demolish the false security of descent: God can raise true children of Abraham even from stones — the Gentiles — so that birth avails nothing without fruit. Jerome's reading of the stones and the moderns' on the Jews' ecclesiastical pride together strike at the root of presumption (v.10).

Birth from Abraham avails nothing without the fruit of repentance, for God can raise true children even from stones (v.10).

The Gentiles, raised by grace, would prove true children of the faith of Abraham (Rom 4:16).
10:
"And now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees: therefore every tree which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire." The witnesses read the announcement of imminent judgment.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the image and the tense. Robertson: "the axe lies at (before) the root of the trees... ready for business" — "the prophetic present" recurring with "hewn down" and "cast." Ellicott: "already — the present of an act no longer future," the symbolism of trees as nations and characters drawn from Isaiah 5:1-7. Lightfoot reads the destruction as proceeding "from the Romans," the axe "now laid to the root."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke presses the urgency: "there is not a moment to spare — God is about to cut off every impenitent soul; you must therefore either turn to God immediately, or be utterly and finally ruined." Wesley pictures "a woodman that has laid down his axe to put off his coat, and then immediately goes to work to cut down the tree."

The Reformed voice reads the sifting of the Church. Calvin: God "is just about to drive out unworthy persons from the Church, as barren" trees. So the witnesses agree: John warns that judgment is not distant but already poised — the axe at the root — so that every fruitless tree faces the fire. The threat presses the demand for fruit (v.8) to its sharpest point: repent now, for the judgment that will fall on the impenitent nation is at hand, and only good fruit will spare the tree (v.12).

So the witnesses sharpen the warning to its keenest point: the axe is not distant but already at the root, and every fruitless tree faces the fire. The moderns' prophetic present and Lightfoot's reading of the Roman judgment, with Clarke's 'not a moment to spare,' together press the demand for fruit into an urgent call to repent now (v.12).

Only good fruit will spare the tree, for the judgment that will fall on the impenitent nation is already at hand (v.12).
11:
"I indeed baptize you with water unto repentance... he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost, and with fire." The witnesses read John's contrast between his water-baptism and Christ's baptism of the Spirit.

The Latin and Eastern fathers fix the difference and the humility. Gregory: "John baptizes not with the Spirit but with water, because he had no power to forgive sins." Chrysostom: "while as yet the sacrifice had not been offered, nor the Spirit descended, how could sin be forgiven?" Remigius lists "five points in which Christ comes after John — his birth, preaching, baptism, death, and descent into hell." Jerome on "whose shoes I am not worthy to bear": "here his humility, there" (the loosing) "his ministry" — "Christ is the Bridegroom, and John" the friend.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the higher baptism. Robertson: "his baptism is water baptism, but the Coming One will baptize in the Holy Spirit and fire" — "Spirit and fire are coupled with one preposition as a double baptism." Ellicott: John's baptism, even for the faithful, "did not go beyond repentance."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the promise: "he shall fill you with the Holy Ghost, inflaming your hearts with that fire of love" — "done, even with a visible appearance as of fire, on the day of Pentecost" (Acts 2:3). So the witnesses agree: John knows himself but the herald — baptizing with water unto repentance, unworthy to bear the Bridegroom's shoes — while the mightier Christ will baptize with the Holy Ghost and fire, the gift that only his finished work can bestow (v.12).

So the witnesses set John's water-baptism against Christ's baptism of the Spirit and fire: the herald, unworthy to bear the Bridegroom's shoes, points to the mightier One. The fathers' that water cannot forgive sins, and Wesley's that the fire of love fell at Pentecost (Acts 2:3), together magnify the gift only Christ's finished work can give (v.12).

John knows himself but the herald, unworthy to bear the Bridegroom's shoes, while the mightier Christ bestows the Spirit (v.12).

Only Christ's finished work could bestow the gift the forerunner foretold.
12:
"Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and gather his wheat into the garner; but he will burn up the chaff with unquenchable fire." The witnesses read the image of the Messiah's judgment, sifting wheat from chaff.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scene. Robertson: "the threshing floor, the fan, the wheat, the garner, the chaff, the fire furnish a life-like picture," the "fire here probably judgment by and at the coming of the Messiah." Ellicott pictures "the large hardened surface" of the eastern threshing-floor, "the winnowing fan driving on them the full force of the strong current of air." Barnes: "a well-known agricultural instrument... to separate grain from the chaff."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke reads a historical edge: "the Romans are here termed God's fan, as in v.10 they were called his axe," and at 22:7 "his armies."

The Reformed voice reads the double message. Calvin: having preached "the grace of Christ" (v.11), John "now discourses of judgment, that he may strike despisers with terror," since "there are always many hypocrites who proudly reject the grace of Christ." So the witnesses agree: the Messiah comes not only to baptize with the Spirit but to judge — fan in hand, gathering the wheat to the garner and burning the chaff with unquenchable fire. The same Christ who saves the penitent will sift and consume the impenitent, so that grace offered and judgment threatened stand together in the forerunner's preaching (v.13).

So the witnesses join grace and judgment in the forerunner's preaching: the Messiah comes both to baptize with the Spirit and to sift, gathering wheat to the garner and burning the chaff. Calvin's that John turns from grace to terror for the despisers, and Clarke's on the Romans as God's fan, together hold the two notes of the Baptist's message (v.13).

The same Christ who saves the penitent will sift and consume the impenitent, grace offered and judgment threatened standing together (v.13).

So the forerunner's preaching holds before the nation both the offered grace and the threatened fire.
13:
"Then cometh Jesus from Galilee to Jordan unto John, to be baptized of him." The witnesses read the coming of the sinless One to a baptism of repentance, and ask why.

The Latin and Eastern fathers give the classic answers. Augustine: "the Saviour willed to be baptized not that he might himself be cleansed, but to cleanse the water for us." Jerome: "that by being himself baptized, he might sanction the baptism of John." Ambrose: "when the Saviour was washed, then the water was cleansed for our baptism." Chrysostom: "with the servants the Lord, with the criminals the Judge, cometh to be baptized," his exaltation shining in his humiliation. Hilary: John would "reject him as God," but Christ teaches it "ought to be performed on him as man." Rabanus notes he came at thirty, the age for priesthood (Numbers 4:3).

The grammatical-historical readers fix the journey. Robertson: "he comes all the way from Galilee to Jordan to be baptized by him," the fame of John having reached Nazareth. The Puritan Henry: after "a long and dark" obscurity, "now... Jesus" comes forth.

The Reformed voice reads the solidarity. Calvin: Christ "received the same baptism with us, in order to assure believers that they are ingrafted into his body, and buried with him" (Romans 6:4). So the witnesses agree: the sinless Christ comes to John's baptism not for cleansing but to consecrate the water, sanction John's ministry, and join himself to sinners — accepting as man what he needed not as God, that he might fulfil all righteousness (v.15).

So the witnesses answer why the sinless One sought baptism: not to be cleansed but to consecrate the water, sanction John's ministry, and join himself to sinners. The fathers' that he cleansed the water for us and Calvin's that he assures believers of their ingrafting into him (Rom 6:4) together unfold the humility of the Lord standing with the servants (v.15).

He accepts as man what he needed not as God, that he might fulfil all righteousness and stand with the people he came to save (v.15).
14:
"But John forbad him, saying, I have need to be baptized of thee, and comest thou to me?" The witnesses read the Baptist's reverent refusal — and his recognition of who stands before him.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the force. Robertson: "tried to prevent" (conative imperfect) — "the two men of destiny are face to face for the first time apparently," and John "recognizes him before the promised sign is given." Clarke: "earnestly and pressingly opposed him," the verb strengthened. Ellicott raises the question how John knew him — whether by previous acquaintance or special inspiration.

The Reformed voice settles the knowledge. Calvin: "John acknowledged Christ to be not only a distinguished prophet... but the Son of God, as he really was; for otherwise he would have dishonoured God by lowering his holy calling to a mortal man." Gill: John "knew Christ before he baptized him," the descending Spirit being not the first signal but the confirmation of his knowledge.

The grammatical-historical Barnes paraphrases John's heart: "it is more fit that I should be baptized with thy baptism, the Holy Ghost, than that thou shouldest be baptized in water by me. I am a sinner, and unworthy to administer this to the Messiah." So the witnesses agree: John, knowing Jesus to be the sinless Son of God, shrinks from baptizing him — confessing his own need and unworthiness, and reversing the proper order, that the greater should receive from the less. His reluctance honours Christ even as it must yield to him (v.15).

So the witnesses read John's refusal as reverence: knowing Jesus the sinless Son of God, he shrinks from baptizing him, confessing his own need. The moderns' that he recognized him before the sign and Calvin's that he knew him as the Son, not merely a prophet, together show the forerunner honouring Christ even as he must yield to him (v.15).

John reverses the proper order, that the greater should receive from the less, his reluctance honouring Christ even as it must yield (v.15).

His shrinking honours Christ even as it must give way to the Lord's higher purpose.
15:
"Suffer it to be so now: for thus it becometh us to fulfil all righteousness." The witnesses read Christ's answer, which overrules John's refusal and explains his purpose.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the words. Robertson: "it was proper to do so, else the Messiah would seem to hold aloof from the Forerunner; thus the ministries of the two are linked together." Ellicott: "the 'now' is emphatic — at the present time, in contrast with what was to follow," for hereafter John should be "the receiver and not the giver." Clarke: "all righteousness" means "every righteous ordinance."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the mutual fitness: "it becometh us (me to receive baptism, and you to administer it)... that I may fully perform every part of the divine will." Barnes: there was "no particular precept" requiring it, but Christ "chose to give the sanction of his example."

So the witnesses agree: Christ does not deny John's unworthiness but waives it "for now," submitting to baptism as a fitting act of obedience that fulfils all righteousness — sanctioning John's ministry, identifying himself with the people he came to save, and setting the seal of his own example on the ordinance. The forerunner yields, and the baptism proceeds, to be crowned at once by the opened heaven and the Father's voice (v.16).

So the witnesses read Christ's 'suffer it now' as an act of obedience that fulfils all righteousness — sanctioning the ordinance, identifying with sinners, setting the seal of his example. The moderns' that the two ministries are linked and Wesley's that every part of the divine will be performed together make the baptism a fitting fulfilment, crowned at once by the opened heaven (v.16).

The forerunner yields, and the baptism proceeds, to be crowned at once by the opened heaven and the Father's voice (v.16).

The act of obedience sanctions the ordinance and seals Christ's solidarity with sinners, that all righteousness might be fulfilled in him (v.16).

The Lord stands among the servants, that he might lead them up out of the water with himself.
16:
"And Jesus, when he was baptized, went up straightway out of the water: and, lo, the heavens were opened... and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove." The witnesses read the opened heaven and the descending Spirit.

The Latin fathers read the figure for our baptism. Ambrose and Rabanus: as the Lord's body dedicated "the laver of baptism," so "the entrance to heaven is open" to the baptized, and the Holy Spirit given. Jerome: the heavens opened "not by an actual cleaving of the visible element, but to the spiritual eye." Remigius asks whether heaven was first opened to Christ's human nature here. Augustine: "as we adore him then born of a pure mother, so we may now receive him" in the sacrament.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the dove. Robertson: it is uncertain "whether the Spirit of God took the form of a dove or came upon Jesus as a dove comes down"; Luke (Luke 3:22) has "in bodily form as a dove." Barnes: "out of the water" means "he went up directly from the water," the heavens opened while he prayed (Luke 3:21).

The Reformed voice reads the manifestation. Calvin: it means "a manifestation of heavenly glory," John seeing "something beyond the planets and stars." Wesley draws the lesson of obedience to divine institutions. So the witnesses agree: at Christ's baptism heaven opens and the Spirit descends as a dove — the first public anointing of the Messiah for his ministry, the heavens parted over the One who had joined himself to sinners, and the gift of the Spirit visibly resting on him (v.17).

So the witnesses read the opened heaven and the descending dove as the Messiah's first public anointing: the Spirit resting on the One who had joined himself to sinners. The fathers' that his baptism dedicates the laver for us and the moderns' care over the dove's form or manner together mark the heavens parting over the consecrated Son (v.17).

The heavens part over the One who had joined himself to sinners, and the gift of the Spirit visibly rests on him (v.17).
17:
"And lo a voice from heaven, saying, This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased." The witnesses read the Father's testimony — and the manifestation of the Trinity at the Jordan.

The Latin fathers see the Triune God revealed. Jerome: "the mystery of the Trinity is shewn in this baptism — the Lord is baptized, the Spirit descends as a dove, the voice of the Father is heard." Ambrose: "no wonder that the mystery of the Trinity is not wanting to the Lord's laver." Pseudo-Augustine: "though Father, Son, and Holy Ghost are one nature, yet... they be Three Persons," the Father alone saying "this is my beloved Son." Augustine: "not as before by Moses and the Prophets... but openly" the Father shows the Son "already come." Remigius (on "in whom I am well pleased"): "whom alone I have found without sin."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the testimony. Robertson: "the voice of the Father to the Son... thus each person of the Trinity is represented" at "this formal entrance of Jesus upon his Messianic ministry." Barnes: the same voice was repeated at the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:5; 2 Peter 1:17) and before his death (John 12:28-30). Lightfoot: Christ honoured "with a threefold testimony... according to his threefold office."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: "a glorious manifestation of the ever-blessed Trinity — the Father speaking, the Son spoken to, the Holy Ghost descending." So the witnesses agree: the baptism closes with the Father's own word naming Jesus his beloved Son — the whole Trinity manifest together, the Son anointed and divinely accredited as he enters his public ministry (Matthew 4:1).

So the witnesses hear in the Father's voice the manifestation of the whole Trinity: Son baptized, Spirit descending, Father testifying. The fathers' 'one nature, three Persons' and the moderns' that this accredits Jesus at his Messianic entrance together make the Jordan the open declaration of who Jesus is, before he enters the wilderness of trial (Matthew 4:1).

The Son is anointed and divinely accredited as he enters his public ministry, the whole Trinity manifest together at the Jordan (Matthew 4:1).
